sentences.text: Still light in that corner.
Kind of brighten up that corner a little.
My name is Roman Ziegler.
I was born on November 26, 1927 in Dabrowa Gornicza, Poland.
It's province of Silesia.
It's D-A-B-R-O-W-A.
Gornicza is G-O-R-N-I-C-Z-A.
My father's name was Simcha.
My mother's was Dvora.
Grossman.
My father had an extensive family.
I really don't know all of them.
I knew two of his brothers.
They were younger brothers.
One survived, and he went from Germany to Israel.
He died in Israel in 1973.
His name was Wolf, or Ze'ev.
The other brother was Schmiel, or Samuel.
But I don't know their names.
One apparently was in Argentina, but I don't remember his name.
Most likely because he was much older.
No.
I do not.
My mother had two brothers.
One that immigrated the United States before the first World War.
And the other perished under the Nazis.
No.
They all lived out of town.
My father and my mother were the only ones.
My mother had also a sister living in Dabrowa.
Yes.
I had three brothers and four sisters.
I was the youngest of eight children.
I am the sole survivor of that family.
My oldest was Gitte Leah, Chaya Sara, Masha, Moishe Nukhem, Hersh David, Jacob, Bella Mindl-- and myself, Roman.
She was born in 1913.
As far as I believe.
After her was Chaya Sara, which was born 1914.
Masha, 1915.
Moishe Nukhem, 1916.
Hersh David, 1918.
Jacob, 1920.
Bella Mindl was born 1924.
Myself, 1927.
My father was in the roofing business and tinsmith, or sheet metal work.
Was self-employed.
No, he was just in Dabrowa.
His customers were individual private owners of homes and some companies.
No.
Predominantly Christian.
Not that I know of.
She was a housewife.
My oldest brother worked with my father.
And the others were in school.
During the war, of course, they worked various jobs.
Well, I was born into a traditional Jewish family.
Middle class.
As I mentioned before, my father was self-employed.
I was attending public school, which was Catholic school.
The town was about 10% Jewish.
And there were some Germans.
And some-- the majority were Poles because we lived in the proximity of the German border, the province of Silesia, Germany.
In fact, you could go from our town by street car into Germany.
It was one huge industrial complex, the steel and coal industry, where we lived.
Now, I attended public school, mornings, which was 8:00 till 1:00.
And afternoon, 2:00 to 5:00, I would go to Hebrew school-- or Jewish school, where I learned Hebrew, the Old Testament, and reading, writing.
And it was very uneventful.
The neighborhood was-- I would call it anti-Semitic.
It was anti-Semitic.
It was anti-Semitic.
It wasn't unusual for a teacher in public school, when he got annoyed he would call you a dirty Jew.
That was quite common.
Also, it happened to me very seldom.
I was one of the favorite students.
I was good at school, In particular, in arithmetic, history.
Whatever I studied, I excelled.
So I won prizes for my studies and acknowledgement.
Yes.
Well, that was part of our life.
You were used to it, and you took it for granted.
Well, kids on the street, for instance, on the way to school or back from school.
Was not uncommon to call you, hey you dirty Jew, thing.
Sometimes even throw a rock at you.
Something like--
Dirty Jew.
That was all.
Or Christ killer.
Yes.
There was one synagogue and there were several prayer houses-- what we used to call [NON-ENGLISH].
And as far as organizations, the Jewish people were pretty well organized.
We had a Jewish community, a community center, similar to what we have here, but not to the extent as here.
And there was a Jewish school also in our town.
And various organizations from the extreme left to the extreme right.
I, as a boy of nine, belonged already to one of the organizations.
I belonged to the Betar, which was a rightist organization.
Or the--
Which is the [NON-ENGLISH], as you would call it today.
The leader, which was Jabotinsky.
Yes, I suppose that a dream of every Jew in Poland-- to get out of there.
I should say Palestine, actually.
And to go to Palestine.
Unfortunately, that was practically impossible.
Yes.
All of them belonged to organizations.
No, other organizations.
My oldest brother belonged to the Halutz.
And others also belonged more to the socialist organizations.
Yes, absolutely.
My parents were part of the Jewish community.
Just law-abiding Jews.
Contributed whenever they could and as much as they could.
Predominantly Polish Catholics, because we happened to live in a Polish district.
Like most of the Jewish towns in Poland, we'd have a concentration of Jewish people where they lived almost like a ghetto.
Certain streets were predominantly Jewish.
And a majority of them would even speak with a distinct Jewish accent, like older bilingual people.
For instance, even like the French in Canada.
When they speak English, you can find that they are a bilingual people.
They speak with a French accent.
And that was the case with the majority of Jews in Poland.
I happen to speak Polish flawless.
Because, as I said, we lived in a Polish district and most of my friends were Poles, Christians.
No.
Very seldom those that I play with.
Occasionally.
I usually used to fight my own battles.
It was on many occasions that I would come home with a bloody nose.
But I fought my own battles, and I gave somebody bloody hoses, too.
If they called me, I hit back.
And I was respected by them.
My friend said, Roman is not like the other Jews.
When somebody calls him a Jew, he hits him right in the face.
And that was-- used to be the case.
My parents had friends, predominantly Jewish people.
Yes.
Neighbors, immediate neighbors.
Yes, on one occasion where my father got a contract from one of his clients-- a Pole, a Christian.
And he mentioned to him, he says, Mr.
Ziegler, I had another offer, which was a bit lower than yours.
But I'd rather give you the job than to the Jew-- not knowing that my father was a Jew, also.
And my father remarked, well, and I thought that he was one of the decent ones.
And yet, eventually came out with it.
No.
He took the job, yes.
Jobs were pretty scarce those days.
How did it begin to change?
It was shortly before the war.
They started to pick at Jewish businesses.
Going actually with signs, don't buy from a Jew.
And signs up here in the Christian stores-- this Aryan business.
In Polish they call it, [POLISH], or Christian or [POLISH], Christian business.
And there were pickets walking in front of the Jewish stores with signs, don't buy from the Jew.
There was another law introduced that every Jew had to have his name on the sign over his store.
Like, for instance, if it was a bakery or a bookstore, it had to be owned by so-and-so.
And had to be to the full Hebrew name written on it-- if he had a first name.
Because most second names were of German origin, which they could not tell.
Like my own name, which is purely German.
But if the father's name was Simcha, you had to have Simcha written on it.
Which the Poles would identify right away as a Jew.
Well, it was a decree from the government, yes.
I'm really too young to know the whole implications of it, how it came about.
But I know the end results.
I remember it clearly.
Unfortunately, the Jews had very little power in Poland to protest or to do something about.
They were a minority.
They probably did.
But it was to no effect.
If fact, one of the ministers-- or the chief ministers, the countries said, yes, to abuse Jews, to kill, I think is forbidden.
But to boycott is permitted.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was a famous saying.
[NON-ENGLISH] is what they said.
Yes.
Yes, we were aware of it.
We weren't in touch-- we read the newspapers.
And it showed people traveling and coming.
You know, we were not restricted to stay in town.
There were people still traveling from town to town, whether it was business people, salespeople, and so forth.
So we were aware of what was going on.
Approximately.
Approximately.
And how did it progress from there?
It progressed almost to the day the war broke out.
It was 1939.
It was no particular drastic increase in it, but those were the laws and you learned to live with them.
People were protesting.
There were Hebrew signs out and so on.
Was business affected?
I suppose it was.
Because even those that didn't mind buying from a Jew didn't feel like crossing the pickets and going into a Jewish store.
They felt self-conscious about it.
Not in particular.
Not In particular.
I lived in a closed circle, so it didn't show as much, especially youngsters and they're 10, 11, 12 year old, so I didn't notice as much.
Not in particular.
I was, I suppose talk about politics and think I was considered too young to be invited to discuss those things.
Not really.
I recall a certain episode in school that was shortly before the war-- and we were taking junior physics.
And we were asked a question.
I think it was about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wedges and things we were studying.
And the teacher put a question to us saying, who can explain?
My mother had two knives-- one was extremely sharp, the other one was dull.
Yet, she preferred to use the dull knife.
She said it was easier to handle.
Nobody could answer that question.
I raised my fingers, say, and he right away remarks, he says, as usual, Ziegler will have the answer to it.
I go up-- just in order to answer that question, I have to go to the blackboard and draw on it.
And I drew two wedges-- one at angle very acute, and the other a large angle-- obtuse.
I said the sharp knife at obtuse angle, or a large angle.
Now, to cut something means to separate two bodies.
So, obtuse angle, in order to separate it requires more force.
Even the knife that wasn't sharp was extremely thin.
And it's easily penetrated to separate the two bodies.
He grabbed me by the head, this time with both his hands.
He says, there is another Einstein growing.
Even if-- so he's a Jew, he will be a good citizen for our country.
Even as a child-- at this time I wasn't 12, yet-- 11 and a half, and it stuck me right away.
And I said, even though I am a Jew, by being a Jew, am I a criminal?
I thought so to myself, when I came home and I repeated the question to my mother and father.
I told him, he says, well, what do you want?
This is Poland.
They don't know any better.
It's how they were raised, and that's how they think.
Was there any reaction from the students in the class?
No.
No reaction from the students.
They also looked up to me that I knew the answers.
My parents?
I think since right after the First World War.
My mother was from Olkusz, and I think that's where they lived after they married.
My father was from a town in [NON-ENGLISH], I believe, which was, oh, I would say about 100 miles away from where we lived.
Well, there was industry, I think.
And that's why.
Our town was a fairly new town by European standards.
It's was originally-- about shortly before the turn of the century, became a settlement when they discovered the coal there.
The villagers from the near-bound villages used to move in, they started to work in the coal mines.
Eventually, it became a city.
But that was before my times.
Right after the turn of the century it became already a city.
It was about 50,000, the population of our town.
No.
My grandparents--
I never knew my grandparents.
Because both my grandfathers were dead before I was born.
And the grandmothers lived far away, 100, so I never knew them 'til they died.
Then she died when I was a little boy, about four years old, five.
So I never knew my grandparents.
No.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I know stories about my maternal grandfather.
He was one of the Jews that was kidnapped as a boy into the Russian army, because central Poland was under Russian occupation before the First World War.
And he spent 15 years and-- almost 16 years in the Russian army.
That was-- they were serving, at that time, 25 years, in the Russian army under the czar.
But he was 15 years in active duty and 10 months on the front line, which was the Turko-Russian War, after 1860.
Sorry '70-- The famous Charge of the Light Brigade, where the British helped the Russians.
He was in that war.
And for each month in the war, he was accounted a year of peacetime duty.
He was discharged and he was given a grant of land from the czar.
And also a watch-- pocket watch.
That was anyone that served 25 years with the czar received that.
That I remember from the stories my mother told me about her father.
It was a cottage.
It was a big building, one.
We lived in a joining building in the back of the yard, which was a cottage-- two-room cottage.
By European standards, that was already middle class if you had two rooms.
Where poor families were living in one room.
It was a kitchen with a coal-burning stove, which we used also in the winter and the second room you'd put in a cast iron store in the winter to heat it, with a pipe going out.
Everything was burned with coal, Like I said, we came from coal mining town.
Where the villages outside were-- would have wood burning stoves, we burned coal.
And toilets were outside.
Water was outside.
There was no running water in those days in the Polish towns.
It was just pumps outside that you had.
It was already city water that we had, not wells.
But there that was just the pump outside, which you pressed and put water.
In the winter it used to be insulated, so the water wouldn't freeze.
There was a bedroom, there were a couple of book cases.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There three beds.
They were folding beds, you know?
[LAUGHS]
We had 10 people.
Well, most the time, like oldest sisters worked out of town, or the neighboring town.
So they had rooms there where they lived.
Most of the people liked to work the salespeople in stores.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And the others stretched out on folding cots, sleeping in the kitchen.
That was the life in Poland.
It's not like here where every child has a room for itself.
It's pretty well hard to understand it when you look from a standard of living that is centuries ahead of what it was in rural Poland.
Fairly good.
Satisfactory I would say.
Not a high standard, but satisfactory.
I had clothes on my back.
I didn't go hungry.
I went to school.
That was-- we were satisfied.
Yes.
Our next door neighbor-- well-to-do.
They got the chemical factory from-- they had tar and roofing supplies, roofing paper.
Their name was Schkowsky.
I met one of their granddaughters-- twins-- that used to play with me when we were children.
I used to love to go to their house.
And we used to slide down the banister down the stairs-- which was a wide mahogany banister.
Slide down.
I still remember it like today.
Yes.
Yes.
He had two employees.
One was Jewish.
And one was a Pole, non-Jew.
